crossref.abstract: <jats:title>Abstract</jats:title><jats:p>Polarimetric infrared (IR) detection bolsters IR thermography by leveraging the polarization of light. Optical anisotropy, i.e., birefringence and dichroism, can be leveraged to achieve polarimetric detection. Recently, giant optical anisotropy is discovered in quasi‐1D narrow‐bandgap hexagonal perovskite sulfides, A<jats:sub>1+</jats:sub><jats:italic><jats:sub>x</jats:sub></jats:italic>TiS<jats:sub>3</jats:sub>, specifically BaTiS<jats:sub>3</jats:sub> and Sr<jats:sub>9/8</jats:sub>TiS<jats:sub>3</jats:sub>. In these materials, the critical role of atomic‐scale structure modulations in the unconventional electrical, optical, and thermal properties raises the broader question of the nature of other materials that belong to this family. To address this issue, for the first time, high‐quality single crystals of a largely unexplored member of the A<jats:sub>1+</jats:sub><jats:italic><jats:sub>x</jats:sub></jats:italic>TiX<jats:sub>3</jats:sub> (X = S, Se) family, BaTiSe<jats:sub>3</jats:sub> are synthesized. Single‐crystal X‐ray diffraction determined the room‐temperature structure with the <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>31<jats:italic>c</jats:italic> space group, which is a superstructure of the earlier reported <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>6<jats:sub>3</jats:sub>/<jats:italic>mmc</jats:italic> structure. The crystal structure of BaTiSe<jats:sub>3</jats:sub> features antiparallel <jats:italic>c</jats:italic>‐axis displacements similar to but of lower symmetry than BaTiS<jats:sub>3</jats:sub>, verified by the polarization dependent Raman spectroscopy. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy is used to characterize the optical anisotropy of BaTiSe<jats:sub>3</jats:sub>, whose refractive index along the ordinary (<jats:italic>E</jats:italic> ⊥ <jats:italic>c</jats:italic>) and extraordinary (<jats:italic>E</jats:italic> ‖ <jats:italic>c</jats:italic>) optical axes is quantitatively determined by combining ellipsometry studies with FTIR. With a giant birefringence Δ<jats:italic>n</jats:italic> ∼ 0.9, BaTiSe<jats:sub>3</jats:sub> emerges as a new candidate for miniaturized birefringent optics for mid‐wave infrared to long‐wave infrared imaging.</jats:p>
title: Infrared Optical Anisotropy in Quasi‐1D Hexagonal Chalcogenide BaTiSe<sub>3</sub>
